title: The UK bee year
subtitle: A calendar of what the colony is doing month by month and what a beekeeper does in response.
excerpt: Bees do not observe a human calendar, but in the UK their annual cycle is predictable enough to plan around. Understanding what the colony is doing in each season shapes every inspection decision: when to add space, when to control swarming, when to harvest, and when to treat for varroa. This reading maps the colony year from January to December.
sourceNotes:
National Bee Unit, Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024 edition), seasonal management sections. https://www.nationalbeeunit.com
British Beekeepers Association syllabus and seasonal guidance notes.
Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Chapter II on the season, Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: The tight ball of bees that forms in the hive from October through to March, with the queen at the centre. The cluster generates heat by muscle vibration, shivering. Bees on the outer shell rotate inwards as they cool.

The rapid increase in population that begins when the first pollen flows arrive in late February and March. The queen's laying rate accelerates as the days lengthen and workers begin bringing in protein from catkins and early spring flowers.

The period, usually May to July in the UK, when field crops (oilseed rape, white clover, lime, field beans) and garden plants produce nectar faster than the colony can take it in. The surplus goes into the supers.

Varroa destructor, the external parasitic mite of honey bees. Reproduces inside capped brood cells, feeding on developing pupae. Left unmanaged, varroa will kill a colony within two to three years. Treatment is part of every UK beekeeper's annual calendar.

content.text: A honey-bee colony in the UK never stops entirely. The

is still generating heat, still consuming winter stores, still has a queen laying at a reduced rate from December in many years. What changes season by season is the direction the colony's energy is pointed: building up, swarming, storing, or surviving.

The colony is in cluster. The beekeeper does not open the hive: lifting the crown board on a cold January day chills the cluster and does more harm than good. Heft the hive occasionally to check the winter stores are not running out. If the hive feels light, feed with fondant placed over the crown-board hole above the cluster, not syrup, which the bees cannot ripen at these temperatures.

begins when pollen arrives from willows, crocuses, and early blossom. The first inspection of the year happens when daytime temperatures are reliably above 12°C: check the cluster is healthy, the queen is laying, and stores are adequate. Add a super early if the colony is already strong, before the oilseed rape flow starts in April.
Swarm season. The colony is at or near peak population and the
is building. Weekly inspections are essential through this period: each one checks for swarm cells at the bottom of combs. The beekeeper adds supers ahead of need, not behind.

The white clover flow peaks and then eases. In most UK regions the main honey flow is over by late July. Supers that are capped or mostly capped can be taken off for extraction. The beekeeper begins thinking about autumn: the
count, the winter-store position, whether the colony needs requeening.

The colony begins to contract. The queen's laying rate drops as days shorten. Heather moorland beekeepers move hives in August for the late ling-heather flow; others in lowland England are already in the wind-down. Autumn feeding begins in late August to early September, targeting 15 to 20 kg of sealed stores in the brood box before October.
The colony goes into cluster as night temperatures drop. The mouse-guard goes on the entrance to stop mice nesting over winter in the warmth of the hive. The oxalic acid
treatment goes on in December when the colony is broodless. The beekeeper's job for the next three months is to leave the colony alone but monitor weight.
